panel.origin: A quick, straight punch thrown with the lead hand. The most fundamental punch — used to measure distance, set up combinations, and keep opponents at bay.
panel.mixology: He kept his opponent off balance all night with a stiff, constant jab.
panel.examples: From 'jab' (a sharp, abrupt poke). The lead-hand jab is boxing's most-thrown and most-important punch, the foundation of nearly every offense.

panel.origin: A powerful straight punch thrown with the rear hand, crossing the body. Often paired with the jab in the classic 'one-two' combination.
panel.mixology: She snapped a jab, then drove the cross straight down the middle for the knockdown.
panel.examples: Named because the rear hand travels 'across' the body to the target. As the 'two' in the one-two, it's a fighter's bread-and-butter power punch.

panel.origin: A punch thrown in a horizontal arc, bent at the elbow, targeting the side of the head or body. Devastating when it lands clean on the chin.
panel.mixology: He ducked the jab and came back with a vicious left hook to the temple.
panel.examples: From the 'hook' shape of the bent arm and curving path. The hook's sideways angle slips around an opponent's guard to land on the jaw or ribs.

panel.origin: A vertical punch thrown upward, traveling under an opponent's guard to land on the chin. Especially effective at close range.
panel.mixology: As they clinched, she ripped an uppercut up the middle that snapped his head back.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the punch cuts 'up' from below. Its rising path slips inside a high guard, making it a brutal weapon in close, infighting exchanges.

panel.origin: A series of punches thrown in quick succession as a coordinated sequence. Combos overwhelm defenses far better than single shots.
panel.mixology: He unloaded a four-punch combination, the flurry too fast for his opponent to block.
panel.examples: From 'combine' — punches 'combined' into a flowing sequence. Stringing strikes together (the 'combo') is how fighters break through a solid guard.

panel.origin: A punch aimed at the torso rather than the head. Body shots sap an opponent's stamina and will over time — 'kill the body, the head will die.'
panel.mixology: Round after round of body shots wore him down until his hands finally dropped.
panel.examples: Descriptive: a shot to the 'body'. A patient body attack is a classic strategy — the damage accumulates, slowly draining an opponent's energy and resolve.

panel.origin: A looping power punch thrown over the top with the rear hand, arcing down onto the target. A favorite of fighters looking for a knockout.
panel.mixology: The shorter fighter landed a huge overhand right that dropped his taller opponent.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the punch comes 'over' the top in a downward arc. Its looping path can sail over a jab to land flush, a notorious knockout blow.

panel.origin: A 'counterpunch' is thrown immediately in response to an opponent's attack, exploiting the opening their punch creates. The art of the counter-puncher.
panel.mixology: She slipped his jab and countered instantly with a cross to the exposed chin.
panel.examples: From 'counter' (to respond or oppose). Great counter-punchers turn an opponent's offense against them, landing in the gap their attack leaves behind.

panel.origin: The defensive position of the hands and arms protecting the head and body. A 'high guard' shields the head; styles vary from peek-a-boo to the long guard.
panel.mixology: He kept a tight guard, hands high, weathering the storm of punches.
panel.examples: From 'guard' (to protect). The hand-and-arm position is a fighter's first line of defense; different guard styles reflect different defensive philosophies.

panel.origin: A defensive move where a fighter shifts their head just outside the line of a punch, making it miss by inches. Pure economy of movement.
panel.mixology: She slipped the jab by a hair and was already loading her counter.
panel.examples: From 'slip' (to slide smoothly aside). The subtle head movement evades a punch without blocking it, leaving both hands free to counter instantly.

panel.origin: A defensive technique of bending the knees ('bobbing') and moving the upper body in a rolling motion ('weaving') to duck under and avoid punches.
panel.mixology: He bobbed and weaved under the looping hooks, slipping inside to land body shots.
panel.examples: Descriptive of the two motions: 'bobbing' down and 'weaving' side to side. Together they let a fighter duck beneath punches and work into close range.

panel.origin: A defensive move that deflects an incoming punch aside with a small, well-timed movement of the hand, rather than absorbing or fully blocking it.
panel.mixology: She parried his jab with a flick of her glove, redirecting it harmlessly past her face.
panel.examples: From Italian 'parare' (to ward off), via fencing. A parry redirects rather than absorbs the blow, conserving energy and opening counter opportunities.

panel.origin: The skill of moving the feet to control distance, angle, and balance. Great footwork lets a fighter attack and escape while staying balanced.
panel.mixology: His dazzling footwork let him dart in, land, and slide out before any counter could land.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'work' done by the 'feet'. Often called the foundation of everything — a fighter who can't move well can't attack or defend effectively.

panel.origin: A close-quarters hold where fighters grab each other to smother offense, rest, or set up other techniques. In MMA, a key position for strikes and takedowns.
panel.mixology: Tired and hurt, he tied his opponent up in a clinch to survive the round.
panel.examples: From 'clinch' (to grip tightly). In boxing it neutralizes offense and buys recovery time; in MMA the clinch becomes a battleground for knees, elbows, and takedowns.

panel.origin: A fake movement designed to provoke a reaction from an opponent, creating an opening for the real attack. Selling a punch you don't throw.
panel.mixology: He feinted the jab, drew the block, then landed the real shot underneath.
panel.examples: From French 'feindre' (to pretend). A feint is a lie told with the body — faking one action to manipulate the opponent into exposing a target.

panel.origin: An MMA and wrestling technique to bring a standing opponent down to the mat, shifting the fight from striking to grappling. Controls where the fight happens.
panel.mixology: She shot in for a takedown, driving through his hips and slamming him to the canvas.
panel.examples: Descriptive: 'taking' an opponent 'down' to the ground. A successful takedown lets a grappler impose their game and escape a striker's range.

panel.origin: On the ground, the position of the fighter on their back using their legs to control the opponent between them. A defensive position that can also attack.
panel.mixology: Flat on his back, he played an active guard, hunting for a submission from underneath.
panel.examples: Borrowed from grappling: the bottom fighter 'guards' against the top with their legs. Far from helpless, a skilled guard threatens sweeps and submissions.

panel.origin: A dominant ground position where a fighter sits atop a prone opponent's torso, freeing their hands to strike or attack with submissions.
panel.mixology: Once she took mount, she rained down punches from the top with the fight nearly hers.
panel.examples: From 'mount' (to climb on top of). One of grappling's most dominant positions — the top fighter controls the action and the bottom struggles to escape.

panel.origin: A technique — a choke or joint lock — that forces an opponent to surrender ('tap out') or risk injury or unconsciousness. A way to win without striking.
panel.mixology: He locked in a tight armbar, and his opponent had no choice but to submit.
panel.examples: From 'submit' (to yield). A submission ends a fight through technique rather than force, compelling surrender via choke or joint manipulation.

panel.origin: Striking a downed opponent from a top grappling position with punches and elbows. A staple offensive tactic of MMA.
panel.mixology: From side control, she opened up with ground and pound until the referee stepped in.
panel.examples: Descriptive: 'pounding' an opponent on the 'ground'. The phrase captures MMA's blend of grappling control and striking, raining blows from a dominant top position.

panel.origin: A defensive reaction to a takedown attempt, where a fighter throws their legs back and drops their hips to avoid being taken down. The striker's best friend.
panel.mixology: He read the shot early and sprawled hard, stuffing the takedown and staying on his feet.
panel.examples: From 'sprawl' (to spread out). By spreading the legs back and dropping weight onto the attacker, a fighter 'stuffs' the takedown and keeps the fight standing.

panel.origin: The rear-naked choke — a submission applied from behind, wrapping an arm around the opponent's neck to cut off blood flow. One of the most effective chokes.
panel.mixology: She took his back, slid her arm under his chin, and the rear-naked choke ended it.
panel.examples: The 'naked' refers to using no gi/collar (bare arms), applied from the 'rear'. Among the most reliable and feared submissions in all of grappling.

panel.origin: A 'KO' — when a fighter is knocked unconscious or cannot rise before the referee's ten-count. The most decisive way to win a fight.
panel.mixology: One clean overhand right ended the night with a brutal first-round knockout.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the opponent is 'knocked out' of the fight. The KO is boxing and MMA's definitive, dramatic finish — no judges, no doubt.

panel.origin: Technical Knockout — when a fight is stopped because a fighter can't safely continue, though they aren't unconscious. The referee, corner, or doctor calls it.
panel.mixology: The referee waved off the fight for a TKO as the defenseless fighter stopped firing back.
panel.examples: Distinguished from a clean KO: 'technically' a stoppage rather than an unconscious knockout. It protects fighters who are hurt and no longer defending themselves.

panel.origin: When a fight goes the full distance, the winner is determined by the judges' scorecards. The outcome rests on rounds won rather than a finish.
panel.mixology: With no knockout, the bout went to a decision, and the judges' cards decided the winner.
panel.examples: From the judges' 'decision'. When neither fighter finishes the other, scoring each round determines who did enough to earn the nod.

panel.origin: A 'unanimous' decision means all judges agree on the winner; a 'split' decision means they disagree, with the majority deciding. Split decisions signal a close fight.
panel.mixology: It was so close it went to a split decision, two judges for her and one against.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the judges are 'unanimous' (all agree) or 'split' (divided). A split decision marks a razor-thin contest that could have gone either way.

panel.origin: A timed segment of a fight (typically 3 minutes), separated by rest. Bouts run a set number of rounds — championship fights often go twelve in boxing, five in MMA.
panel.mixology: He saved his energy, knowing he had to pace himself across all twelve rounds.
panel.examples: From the sense of a 'round' or cycle of action. Dividing fights into rounds with rest between shapes pacing, strategy, and the scoring of the bout.

panel.origin: The official weighing of fighters before a bout to confirm they meet their weight-class limit. A tense ritual, sometimes more dramatic than the fight.
panel.mixology: He barely made the limit at the weigh-in after a grueling final day of cutting weight.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the fighters are 'weighed in' to the contest. Making weight is its own brutal discipline, and the weigh-in's face-offs build the fight's drama.

panel.origin: To signal surrender by physically tapping the opponent or mat, conceding a submission. The instant, unambiguous way to quit before injury.
panel.mixology: Caught in the armbar, she tapped out before her elbow could give way.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the fighter 'taps' to submit. The tap is grappling's universal surrender signal, ending a fight the moment a submission becomes inescapable.

panel.origin: A wild, looping, all-or-nothing power punch thrown with maximum force and little technique. Dangerous if it lands, reckless if it misses.
panel.mixology: He swung a desperate haymaker that, by luck, caught his opponent flush and ended it.
panel.examples: From the wide, swinging motion resembling scything hay with a tool. A haymaker trades accuracy for raw power — a knockout if it lands, a gamble always.

panel.origin: Slang for a fighter who is easily knocked out, unable to absorb punches to the chin. The opposite of a fighter with a 'good chin.'
panel.mixology: Talented as he was, his glass jaw meant one clean shot could always end his night.
panel.examples: A vivid metaphor: the jaw is as fragile as 'glass', shattering under a blow. A fighter's chin (ability to take a punch) is among their most prized traits.

panel.origin: A fighter pinned against the ring ropes and absorbing punishment, in serious trouble. Figuratively, anyone in a desperate, losing situation.
panel.mixology: She had him on the ropes, teeing off as he covered up and prayed for the bell.
panel.examples: From a fighter literally being trapped against the 'ropes' of the ring, unable to escape. The phrase spread into everyday speech for being in dire straits.

panel.origin: A left-handed fighter who leads with the right hand and stands in a mirror-image stance. Their unusual angles trouble orthodox opponents.
panel.mixology: The orthodox boxer struggled all night against the southpaw's awkward, reversed angles.
panel.examples: Possibly from baseball, where left-handed pitchers faced south in old ballparks, their throwing arm to the 'south'. Southpaws frustrate fighters used to right-handers.

panel.origin: A way of ranking fighters' skill independent of weight class — imagining them all the same size. The mythical answer to 'who's the best, regardless of weight?'
panel.mixology: Though a lightweight, she was considered the best pound-for-pound fighter in the world.
panel.examples: A thought experiment: comparing fighters 'pound for pound' as if equal in size. It honors pure skill, letting a small fighter top a list over giants.

panel.origin: To 'gas out' is to run out of stamina and energy mid-fight, leaving a fighter exhausted and vulnerable. Conditioning's cruelest failure.
panel.mixology: He swung for the fences early, gassed out by the third round, and had nothing left.
panel.examples: From running out of 'gas' (fuel). A fighter who empties the tank too soon 'gasses out', their offense and defense crumbling as exhaustion takes over.
